result:
The quiet is not empty. That’s the first thing. It has weight, texture—a soft, gray wool kind of texture, like the inside of an old pocket. I am sitting, and the world is not asking anything of me for this single, stolen fragment of an hour. The clock on the wall is a liar; it ticks with a mechanical regularity that has nothing to do with the flow of time in here, in this quiet.

Outside, a bird negotiates with the sky. A series of chirps, a questioning arc of sound. It occurs to me that we have forgotten how to speak in arcs. Our communication is angular, efficient, a transfer of data points. “I am here.” “This is what I need.” “This is wrong.” We have built a labyrinth of straight lines and called it connectedness. But in this woolen quiet, I remember the arc. The unsaid, curving space between one person and another that holds the real meaning, like the air in a bell that has just been struck.

There is a crack in the teacup on the desk. A fine, hairline thing, visible only when the light hits the glaze just so. It does not leak. It is not broken, not in the useful sense. But it is changed. Imperfect, and therefore specific. It is this cup, and no other. We spend so much energy avoiding our hairline cracks, plastering over them, holding ourselves together so we can remain generic, functional, un-leaking. But what if the crack is the point? The place where the light gets in, as the poet said, but also the place where our particular story begins? The cup was whole, then it met a moment of pressure, a tap against the sink, and now it carries a map of that encounter. A memory in porcelain. Aren’t we all just collections of such maps?

The light is shifting. It’s a late afternoon light, honey-thick and sentimental. It paints the dust motes into galaxies, swirling in unseen currents. Each mote, a world. Each swirl, a history. We are breathing in universes. I think of all the rooms I have sat in, all the different qualities of silence they held. The strained silence of a hospital hallway, humming with fear. The contented silence between two people reading, a bridge of shared peace. The awe-struck silence under a canopy of stars, so vast it makes your ribs ache. Silence is not one thing. It is a language.

My mind wanders to a river I knew as a child. It was brown and slow, fringed with willowherb. I would spend hours there, a small figure on a muddy bank, hunting for frogsporn or skimming stones. The stones. The perfect, flat, disc-shaped stones. Selecting one was a solemn ritual. You had to judge its weight, its balance, the curve of its edge. Then the throw—a low, side-armed flick of the wrist, the whole body coiling and releasing. A good throw produced not a splash, but a series of rapid *kip-kip-kip* sounds as the stone danced across the surface tension, defying its own nature for a few glorious seconds. The goal was always more skips. But now, I think the beauty was in the defiance itself. A stone is meant to sink. For one arc of its journey, it chooses, or is chosen, to fly. Isn’t that the brief, brave story of everything? We are all stones built to sink, yet we spend our lives perfecting the art of the skip.

The bird outside has concluded its negotiation. The quiet returns, deeper now. It holds the echo of the bird, the memory of the river, the potential of the unsaid words piled up in my throat like unskipped stones. I think about the 1000 words. This assignment. It felt like a cage at first. A limit. But now I see it as the flat stone. These words are the impulse, the flick of the wrist. They are not the meaning. The meaning is in the arc they travel, in the silent spaces between them, in the things they point to but cannot capture: the feel of woolen quiet, the taste of dust-galaxies, the sound of a skip across the water of a childhood afternoon.

We are so afraid of not being profound. Of wasting our words, our time, our single skip across the water. We want to be the perfect stone, the un-cracked cup. But here, in the permission of this quiet, I think the profound is in the noticing. Noticing the crack. Noticing the quality of the light. Noticing that your breath has slowed to match the pace of the ticking liar-clock on the wall.

The honey-light is fading into blue. The quiet is changing its clothes, preparing for the evening. It will become a different kind of quiet—deeper, more introspective, peppered with the distant sounds of owls and settling houses.

I have used some of the words. Not all. There is a space left, a breath at the end of the sentence. That space is important. It is where you, the reader, come in. You bring your own cracks, your own river-stones, your own quality of silence. This was never a monologue. It was always a skip sent out from my bank, hoping to touch the surface of your understanding, to make a few connection points before it sinks into the dark water of finished things.

The cup is just a cup. The quiet is just air. The words are just marks. But for a moment, they held a charge. They became a vessel. They carried the arc. And maybe that’s enough. Maybe that’s everything. To be a vessel, however cracked, for a little while. To hold the light, and then to let it go.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
